content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Sci fi’s like “Contact”

**Score:** 38
**Body:** Just finished Contact… what an awesome film. Really enjoyed it and now I’m looking for other sci fi movies that are similar, preferably “better”. Thanks in advance! 

Other movies I really enjoyed were interstellar and arrival. 

Hard to say what I’m looking for exactly but I really like the subliminal message type of sci fi. Contact and arrival nailed that

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Another Earth
2. Gattaca
3. The Day the Earth Stood Still is a classic from the 50s 
4. For abyss watch the directors cut. The directors cut gives a completely different ending to the movie and is much much better.
5. If you’re open to series, The Expanse is probably the best sci-fi series out there. There’s an underlying subliminal alien messaging theme throughout the series. 
6. The Martian is one of the better films in this genre.
7. Interstellar 

Close Encounters of the Third Kind 

Bugonia 

The Abyss 

2001 A Space Odyssey 

Solaris 1972

Annihilation 

AI Artificial Intelligence 

Moon
  - While it's a great movie, putting Bugonia on this list is kind of a spoiler.  It's also pretty far from Contact, unless your favorite part of Contact was Jake Busey and you just want to learn about that guy's home life.
8. Arrival, but be prepared to cry.

The one with Amy Adams, not Charlie Sheen

Edit: Oops, you already said that
9. Cosmos (2019) might meet the criteria.

## 2. Movies that do something... New?

**Score:** 32
**Body:** I'd like some recommendations for movies that do something new. Something I might not have seen before, or in a way I haven't seen before. No preferred genres if the movie is good, but I'd love to hear why you would recommend it. 

Question was inspired by The Zone of Interest, which was very good.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Good Boy (2025) - home invasion horror movie, shown completely from the family dog's perspective.
  - If I'm honest, I don't think it looks particularly good. But my god if that isn't the most original idea for a movie I've seen, well, ever.
2. Being John Malkovich and Adaptation felt like really new ways of interacting with an audience, for me. 

Nickel Boys is a must see. Told in a way I’d never seen, and a powerful story.
3. Depends how deeply you've dived into the canon, but imo all of the following do something new in a way that becomes pretty obvious. A couple of these are grouped together because it's the same director and the films share similarities (not plot-wise, just in the way the director decided to execute the concepts)

2001: Space Odyssey

Before Trilogy & Boyhood 

Stalker & Andrei Rublev

Duel

Rififi

The Red Shoes

It's Such A Beautiful Day

The Celebration (Danish, 1998)
4. Two completely different recommendations:

1st- it doesn’t necessarily have something new (and perhaps to the contrary, it wasn’t new enough and got overlooked upon release): “Sorcerer”. A masterpiece film with a terrific storyline, acting, and some of the most impressive practical effects in cinema. A gritty film, it was largely overlooked with one reason being released around the same time as Star Wars, where people opted for the fun optimism of the new space adventure. One of Friedkin’s best (if not his best). 

2nd- if you don’t mind subtitles (or if you speak fluent French), dive into the French new wave films of the 60’s. This genre pioneered a completely new take of filmmaking that broke conventional rules of cinema up to that point. Some key directors include Melville, Godard, Truffaut. A lot of these (especially from the latter two directors) are quite obscure and can be tedious to get into, but some more accessible ones included: 400 Blows (Truffaut), Breathless (Godard), A Band Of Outsiders (Godard), and Le Samourai (Melville)- all but Outsiders currently streaming on Max. You’ll be surprised to see how many contemporary directors (including Spielberg and Tarantino) were influenced by these trailblazing French directors. 

Let me know if you dive into any! 
5. Aggro Dr1ft:  Shot entirely with infrared cameras, so the whole movie looks like some strange neon-drenched video game or thermal dream. It’s less about plot and more about the vibe and the visuals.

Mishima: A Life in Four Chapters: A biopic that mixes Mishima’s real life with staged versions of his novels, each with totally different visual styles. It jumps between timelines and fiction in a really creative way.

The Fall: A visually stunning fantasy where a stuntman tells a story to a little girl in a hospital, and the movie shows the story through these huge, imaginative sequences filmed in real locations around the world.
6. Polytechnique - (triggering) - shot in black and white and with a very unique style

Melancholia - one of the weirder movies I have seen. And its love it or hate it in my experience.

MADS - all one take, and a very unique take on the zombie (or not) genre

Bugonia - Just. Weird.  Not sure if funny or weird or sad or scary.

Late Night with the Devil - cool combo of "home footage" and "found footage" with a healthy dose of mystery
7. * Un Chien Andalou (1929)
* Meshes of the Afternoon (1943)
* Memento (2000)
* Tarnation (2003)
* Synecdoche, New York (2008)
* Dogtooth (2009)
* Under the Skin (2013)
  - Also Dogville 
8. Pleasantville 

## 3. I need a good woman empowerment movie

**Score:** 29
**Body:** Hey yall i need a good woman empowerment movie to watch. NO SPOILERS PLEASE 

Ima give yall some examples of woman empowerment movies i loved so you have a sense of what my movie style is. I love movies with plot twists, figth against discrimination, complex characters.   
Promising young woman   
Hidden figures   
Mulan   
Brave   
Legally Blonde   
Barbie   
Harriet  
LITTLE WOMEN   
On the basis of sex 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 9 to 5. I'm a 54 year old guy but I love that movie. Just noticed it was on Peacock today.
2. Erin Brockovich (2000)
3. Thelma and Louise
  - OP-a lot of these others are very good, but this is the one you're looking for. 

Aliens is kind of the first blockbuster female empowerment movie, but Thelma and Louise goes a lot harder while still being an awesome thrill ride. Personally I think it's Ridley's second best after Alien.
4. Contact

Erin Brockovich

A League of Their Own 

The Client


5. Lady Bird 

The other versions of Little Women lol

True Grit 

Juno 

Clueless 

Crouching Tiger Hidden Dragon 

Chicago 

Jennifer's Body 

Frances Ha 

Princess Diaries
6. Shoot!...I was going to suggest Promising Young Woman.  But seems you've already seen it :/

***Maybe?...***

Fried Green Tomatoes

House of Sand and Fog

Steel Magnolias

Aliens

Closer

Silkwood

Annhiliation

Joan of Arc




7. Fury Road!!!! 
  - Furiosa too
8. Atomic Blonde. Red sparrow. 

## 4. HILARIOUS movie to watch with kids?

**Score:** 21
**Body:** Today is my birthday! And I’m about to be going through a divorce.. wanting to watch a crack up movie with my kids (15, 11, and 7). Some language is okay, but want to avoid nudity/sexual content. (Nothing too scarring to watch with your mom 😅)  

We’ve watched:  

Almost all of the Adam Sandler films  

Farley classics like: Beverly Hills Ninja, Black Sheep, Tommy Boy  

All of the old Disney classics (Heavyweights, Cool Runnings, etc)  

2000’s bangers like: What Happens in Vegas, 13 Going on 30, Legally Blonde, etc)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Uncle Buck of course!
2. Watch, Mrs Doubtfire


They'll understand your side of what's coming without you having to talk about it and have fun watching it as well.


3. Totally offbeat movie if you haven't seen the old "documentary" The God's Must be Crazy...
4. The Bad News Bears (1976)
5. school of rock !! one of my favs to this day, and actually has some great messages along with the humor. 
6. Nacho Libre is the only correct answer 
7. The Sandlot 1993

The Princess Bride 1987
8. Some Jim Carey movies, I'd argue kids would like too :) 


  - Dumb & Dumber is a good place to start
9. Inner Space. Its a slightly more adult version of Honry I shrunk the kids, or a funny version of Fantastic Voyage. Martin Short at his best.


I was howling along with my kids at many parts.

## 5. If you are into romantic-adventure-exploration, watch 'Before' Trilogy

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I just completed last movie today . 3 movies . 

\- Before Sunrise  
\- Before Sunset  
\- Before Midnight

For me it is one the best romantic movies i have watched, the entire circle, how beautifully they have made all the movies. Each movie timeline is 9 years apart and i watched them at least a month a part due to time constraint . 

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I saw these for the first time in the past few years, each one while traveling, and they're really excellent and not overrated. The first one swept me off my feet! 
  - Wow bro, you watched it during travel, it must have been one hell of a experience, the first one was special for me as well . 
    - Someone recommended it on Reddit as a train trip movie. It swept me right up
2. Genuinely amazing movies, before sunset is in my top 5 of all time and my favorite movie ending
  - In the last movie, near ending i thought, no no this couldn't happen, they are going down heel and within 5 min they just come back and every thing is back on track . Like it was magic . 
3. A friend rented out a theater a few years ago and we had a group of about 50 of us watch the trilogy. It was my first time seeing them. Absolutely brilliant films!
4. i was expecting a 4th ... it's overdue :(

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Jason Isaacs in The Patriot is one of the most despicable movie villains ever

**Score:** 1312
**Body:** Rewatched The Patriot and I cannot believe how much I hate his character.

Just merciless and a tyrant. Jason Isaacs absolutely crushed it in the role.

Without a doubt, he’s gotta be one of the most hated villains of all time. 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Agreed. That guy is great in everything he's been in. 
  - He absolutely needs to be a Bond villain. 
    - Goodness yes!! I’ve been saying that forever.

He’d be an incredible Bond villain!!
      - His performance as Lucius Malfoy already proved he can do sophisticated, cold-blooded evil perfectly.
2. Tell me about… *Ohio.*
  - Only Jason Isaacs could deliver a line like that and make it sound menacing. 
3. His villainy in the Patriot is only surpassed by his villainy as Lucius Malfoy. He crushed both roles.
  - With you there!

Him and Alan Rickman are some of my faves in the HP franchise. 
  - I don't think Lucius came even close to surpassing his villainy in The Patriot.
4. Yeah he was great. My buddies and I saw it in theaters. Absolutely hated his character. He was also great in death of Stalin as general Zhukov. 
Edit :not stukov that was the StarCraft general. Haha my bad. 

## 2. Blade Runner • 1982 : Possibly the best ever closing monologue?

**Score:** 673
**Body:** Interesting fact :

**Rutger Hauer** who played **Roy Batty** edited the monologue himself a night before. Making it shorter and added the ever famous and heart felt line...."***like tears in rain***". He didn't tell anyone, just acted it out on the camera.

Add to that the amazing cinematography, music by Vangelis and acting, it just couldn't get any better.

Any other cinematic moments from other movies?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Only in the “Definitive Edition”. 

The theatrical version had a very different ending monologue / voice over that was terrible.

But I agree that this version was pretty great.
  - I only watched this version. I know Blade Runner has a lot many versions. Even the soundtrack by the great Vangelis, one of my favourite never got released properly.
    - This version was the only one that requires watching. It’s the legit version. You can skip the others. 🤜🤛
  - I saw the original in theater on opening release and that version had the same dialog, not "completely different".   Yes, the voice over (in multiple places in the film) was terrible, but Batty's end monologue (improvised by Hauer) was the same.
  - Maybe a dumb question but how do I know which cut is on a streaming service? It doesn’t say specifically 
2. Like Tears in Rain (2024) is an award-winning documentary about the life of actor Rutger Hauer, who passed away in 2019. Directed by his goddaughter Sanna Fabery de Jonge, the film offers an intimate portrait and shows what a special person Rutger was.
3. Apex level cinema. Blade Runner stands up as well if not better than any movie its age, in no small part because of Hauer’s performance. 
  - So true.....but that movie has many components that make it so well.

I have read the novel and can truly say that the movie was much better.

Direction, cinematography, music, acting....I mean everything is simply brilliant.
4. enhanced by the (imo) best movie score ever. Vangelis made the music that made the movie. The score doesn't accompany the movie - it's truly embedded. 
5. ... and the subtle perfectly synchronised ascending development in the music... it is there, in typical Vangelis manner, until we become very aware of it at the moment of the dove being released... and the thundering percussive accent on the Blade Runner melodic motif. Sound, image, and text come together perfectly. 

## 3. Ben Foster appreciation post

**Score:** 618
**Body:** Never really comes up in those ‘Best actor working today?’ posts, but he’s right up there for me

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Always elevates. He was the better end of his and Chris Pine's performances in Hell or High Water (and I thought Pine was great), which is just a fucking excellent movie. He was a force of nature in 3:10 to Yuma, and absolutely set the tone in 40 Days of Night, for the 6 whole minutes he was in the movie.
  - What a gem Hell or High Water is
    - “What don’t you want?”
2. Ben Foster’s the guy who makes every supporting role feel like the main event dude steals scenes like it’s a hobby.
3. Six Feet Under!!!
4. Fantastic actor who deserves more recognition 
5. Love him in The Mechanic, 3:10 to Yuma, Lone Survivor, I like him in pretty much everything he's in. I love actors like him, almost never playing the lead role, but always stealing the show.
6. 3:10 to Yuma 
His performance goes toe to toe with Bale and elevates Crowe. We almost never see this from supporting characters.
Could say the same for many of the films he is in. One of the best supporting character actors of our generation 
7. I loved him in Pandorum, which comes in my top 10 best movies of all time. 
8. If you don’t like Ben Foster, you don’t like movies 

## 4. Favourite movie gadget you wish existed in real life??

**Score:** 390
**Body:** Self tying shoes from “Back to the Future Part 2”

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’m 100% sure these exist in real life.
  - I’d take the hover board over these 
  - The very picture OP posted is from real life, and not from the film. 
2. ![gif](giphy|fjpYUp612jgXe)

The gun from Fifth Element of course
3. https://preview.redd.it/ltxpqosmljng1.jpeg?width=474&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=99cb8e0a6eb8fcb9fd5f588474d844defc6b483c

Tell me, who didn’t want a bread-toasting knife from Hitchhiker’s Guide to The Galaxy?
4. ![gif](giphy|jd6TVgsph6w7e)

I know they have toy versions but man to have a real light saber
5. Holodeck.
6. Star Trek replicator
7. That uh gadget in Black Mirror that allows you to feel your partners pleasure too

## 5. What did everyone think of V for Vendetta?

**Score:** 316

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Good, fun, apropos, sometimes too accurate, well written, well adapted, well cast, and well acted.
  - ![gif](giphy|11FiDF2fuOujPG)


  - Keeps getting better with age as the world turns to shit.
2. I think Stephen Fry is one of the best people to ever happen to the entire planet. 
  - So true about him.
3. Scarily relevant in todays world 
4. Another great cautionary tale which went completely ignored.
5. My favorite documentary 
6. Oddly enough it actually reflects a good deal about modern britain today.

But its a really good film, good acting from all included, good action, nice twists and turns and character development.
7. I like it

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Thoughts on a very neat detail in: One Battle After Another

**Score:** 47
**Body:** When I first saw the movie I noticed a rifle featured in Lockjaw’s unit that was pretty crazy to see.

The US military has introduced a new, and very recent rifle that is “meant” (heavy on meant) to replace the M4a1. Think of the gun Val Kilmer shoots in Heat. This rifle is the MCX Spear, otherwise known as the M5. 

I suspected upon seeing it that it was the rifle’s first ever debut on the big screen, so I went to the Movie Firearms Database to check to see if it’s mentioned as such, and it is!

“Some MKU officers, including Colonel Steven J. Lockjaw (Sean Penn) are seen carrying the SIG-Sauer MCX-SPEAR…(gun attachment jargon). According to IMFDB, this is the first screen appearance of the SIG MCX-SPEAR in a feature film.” - MFD

This new rifle is a pretty cutting edge military rifle and pretty controversial, as most weapons or contracts are awarded to the lowest bidders and may not represent the actual needs of soldiers in modern warfare.

I would argue it leans far too heavily into the “battle rifle category” which essentially means it shoots a bigger bullet meant to penetrate armor rather than suppressing, or shooting a lot of bullets to keep your enemy in cover. I would also argue that it will not replace our current rifles anytime soon (we simply have too many of them), and each of the US branches choose their weapons to suit their needs anyway.

This doesn’t mean it doesn’t have its uses. It’s used in the film both as a CQC weapon when the soldiers first enter their humble abode in the forest, and they use it against Leo’s character when he fires at Lockjaw’s unit with a sniper rifle. All of which are very possible in real life. 

It speaks to the films capability of acknowledging how close One Battle After Another is to our current reality. This gun is essentially the near, near future of US military infantry, and it’s in terrifyingly capable hands in the film. It’s always interesting to see military reality be reflected in the big screen, especially accurately. 

Makes me wonder if we can see other films showcasing drone warfare like we’ve seen in Ukraine. One can only imagine how sound design could play into a film showcasing the whirring of drones overhead as a means to build tension or a sense of danger while navigating claustrophobic trenches. I imagine how GoPro’s can turn actors into cameramen reenacting things we see on video in the frontlines. Imagine that: A movie using acted out GoPro footage meant to portray frontline warfare. What can feel realer than a direct imitation of the footage we get on our phones? That blend between stuff we see on the news and entertainment is pretty scary. 

Or what about shots showing the fields full of fiber optic cable from drones moving back and forth along battle lines? How will movies soon adapt to the modern combat climate? How will we soon rationalize modern warfare into something horrifying and pointless, and encourage others to make the same mistakes by portraying it aesthetically? Things I’m thinking about, that’s all! 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Guns have always been a great tool to make a movie more haptic. They tell a lot about their users, the way they are used and handled. Especially OBAA has some great characterisation through firearms, every character has a fitting gun. Overpriced tactical bling, old hunting rifle, etc.. 

I find it kind of strange how a "gun-nut" movie like john wick is probably very accurate hen it comes to guun handling but they all feel like toys or props instead of actual guns. I guess it shows that attention to detail doesn't necessarily equal feeling realistic. 

As for your last point i was wondering if right now we are still in a bit of a shock/ cope phase when it comes to war movies and even videogames. Understandably a lot of people and studios steer clear of this topic right now because its a touchy subject and man lets be honest its a huge bummer. The best war movie of the decade is probably "Warfare" hands down. But its a horrific experience of a movie that most people probably wouldn't actively seek out. Small scale horror instead of spectacle action seems like the way to go for the genre. 
  - John wick guns with CGI muzzle flash, not using traditional blanks (understandably) and guns used at hand to hand combat range almost always comes off as cartoon-like.  At times the actors don't look like they are firing a gun because there is no chemical explosion to react to. 

Might as well be using Star Wars blasters.  




2. This post is very informative - as someone unfortunate enough to enjoy Escape From Tarkov ("realistic" Russian societal collapse economic military simulator first person shooter), I've been familiar with the SPEAR for a couple of years without knowing where it fits into the canon of American military firearms.



3. This is only a side point in your post but drone warfare on film is an entirely different can of worms that I think most people aren’t ready for, I know I’m not. I think any movie “realistically” depicting drone warfare would come off as so cold and so unattached from the violence, that either no one would watch it because it’s too uncomfortable or the public would turn against the entire concept of it with shocking speed.

“Sound whirring of drones overhead to build tension” sounds a lot more like Zone of Interest than any combat-war movie I’ve seen. If you want to make people queasy with an explicit anti-war message, maybe I see the potential, but I don’t see anyone making a cool badass Lone Survivor or Blackhawk Down type movie where yeah the violence is awful, but look at this cool guy with his joystick blowing up buildings at no risk to himself. 
  - I think they meant showing the perspective of soldiers on the ground, trying to hide from the drones. 

Would be anything but unattached from the violence. If anything it’d be uniquely terrifying 
    - it would be a war movie version of the black mirror robot dog episode
  - Good Kill, starring Ethan Hawke, explores the topic of drone warfare. It’s the main focus of the story, so if that interests you, I’d recommend checking it out. 
4. In terms of your last point, I really enjoyed Alex Garland’s Civil War and a lot of the dialogue is about the “point” or lack thereof of the conflict onscreen. The soldiers are there to soldier but everyone else is mostly confused or nihilistic. 
  - I liked Civil War! The first time I watched it I suppose. The second time I was really surprised at how much it fails to distinguish between journalism, its point as a service to the public and war photography. 

It plays a weird role with the purpose of cameras on the film and I think falls into this weird role of voyeurism without a purpose. But I guess that can contribute to your point! 

    - There was a lot being said about it all yes. I think exploring the “bad” side of journalism and journalists was interesting, like how the girl was essentially an adrenalin junkie chasing highs.

Ultimately, yeah, one of the biggest themes I got from a repeat viewing was how callous everyone is and how little value human life is given onscreen. 

## 2. Another Country (1984)

**Score:** 18
**Body:** I'm reading Slavoj Zizek's The sublime object of Ideology and he mentions this film. And I immediately go and watch this fil,  being cautious about how good/bad it could be considering IMHO he overemphasises certain things at times(or maybe I don't get him enough). Nonetheless he's someone I respect and it turned out to be a great little film about a specific time and place(Britain, 1930s) while also hinting about larger themes -loyalty, country, colonialism, being enamoured by communism for what's sake etc.
While doing all this without being epic. It reminded, in elements, about a film called House of Sand and Fog which through a simple film throws nuanced differences about dignity, ethos of west and the east.
Me being a brown person(east) movied to a very white EU country(west) recently makes me respect the film more.
Writing this post to appreciate  these simple films in the corners of filmographies. Another one is from the 80s which I can't remember is just ordinary street gangs or something in an American City (NY or LA) running around and maybe fighting during the nights. I cannot recall but if someone can help..

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. By Communism? 
Are you sure you understand core political ideologies of the past century?
I recommend reading animal farm next.

Ps: If this is rage bait, then enjoy the laugh I guess.

Edit: I'm not entirely sure why you'd call yourself a brown person, anyway, what's the point distinguishing culture according to general skin tone? Are people in your country with lighter skin than you less from from your country than you are?
And nevertheless, civilization of the west and east should also be a thing of the past by now, rule of capital pretty much hoarded every economy and culture in the same sack for the past century to say the least.

Let me recommend "Killing them softly", it is a nice little flick (you seem like the person who would not want to miss something with Bratt Pitt in it)
  - Wow! This response is a masterclass in missing the forest for the trees. Recommending Animal Farm to someone analyzing a film through Slavoj Žižek is peak condescension. But I'm pretty sure you know that. Another Country isn't a brochure for Stalinism; it’s a study of the 1930s Oxbridge Marxist phenomenon, where elite "Old Boys" like the real-life Cambridge Five embraced Communism as a psychological weapon against an establishment that criminalized their identity and sexuality. And dismissing OP’s perspective as a "brown person" moving from East to West as "irrelevant" because of global capital is incredibly out of touch. Moving between cultures provides a specific, necessary lens on belonging and exclusion, which are the literal themes of the film. Telling an immigrant that their cultural friction doesn't exist anymore doesn't make you enlightened; it just means you aren't listening. Calling this "rage bait" while throwing in a lazy Brad Pitt dig just confirms you’re more interested in "winning" an argument than engaging with a nuanced critique of institutional rot.

OP: That 80s gang movie you’re thinking of is almost certainly The Warriors (1979).
    - Yes. It is The Warriors. Thank you Batman !!
    - Sorry, I'm not in very sociable mood right now to read your whole comment. 


Obviously the recommendations I made was to trigger the guy's interest into educating themselves politically, it must be pretty obvious but the way I started off my whole responce (something about trees being missed among a forest).
Upon reading further however, since it's just in front of me, I have to say I don't really use terms like enlightened, unless playfully using sarcarm and yes this is from the start some argument to be won and for the subjects participating to feel a certain remorse for how the currently think and their ethics.
Edit: Assuming someone bothered to take a glimpse at history for the past...fucking forever
      - The fact that you are telling someone who's working through The Sublime Object of Ideology to educate themselves with Animal Farm is laughable.
        - And yet here they are. Same as you and about 18 downvotes that I'm counting so far. 

Long live war, sit at your sofa and sip your tea while little arrow icons represent your participatipation in the global agora.
          - You shouldn't drink so much cough syrup...

## 3. Anyone have suggestions for a dark, atmospheric horror? The rainier the better.

**Score:** 14
**Body:** I’m a huge fan of horror, particularly films in which the weather is a supporting character, and I’m trying to compile a list of similar movies. So far I have on my list: 

* The Ring (2002) / Ringu (1998)
* Underworld (2003)
* Silent Hill (2006)
* Pontypool (2008)
* The Thing (1982) 
* 30 Days of Night (2007)
* Dark Water (2002) / (2005)
* Nosferatu (2024)
* Exhuma (2024)
* Gretel & Hansel (2020)
* Alien Romulus (2024)
* The Black Phone (2021)
* The Lodgers (2017)
* Saint Maud (2019) 
* The Hunting of Hill House (2018)
* The Haunting of Bly Manor (2020)
* Midnight Mass (2021)
* A Fantastic Fear of Everything (2012)
* The Others (2001)
* The Crow (1994)
* Bulbul (2020)

.. and that’s all I can come up with, but I’d love to hear some of your suggestions!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It’s not horror but Dark City is a great film. I won’t spoil any of it for you  but it definitely has the atmosphere you’re looking for. 

I go back and watch it every once in a while. Rufus Sewell is great in it and of course there’s Jennifer Connelly. 
2. I highly recommend Herzog's version of Nosferatu, if you haven't seen it. Even darker and more brooding than Eggers'. And the king of dark, atmospheric horror is David Lynch. Just watch Twin Peaks, that's a lot of hours to keep you busy (scared and disturbed).
3. Other Eggers films like The Lighthouse and potentially The Witch. Also Identity (2003) contains quite a bit of rain too. That's all I can think of for now. Word count, word count, word count
4. The Lodge from 2019, one of the more underrated installments of the “trauma as horror” wave of the 2010s. About a woman who gets stuck in a cabin with her new boyfriend’s two children during a snow storm and crazy stuff starts happening
5. Messiah of Evil...may be fair to say the moon is a supporting character.

(length requirement length requirement length requirement length requirement length requirement length requirement length requirement length requirement length requirement length requirement length requirement length requirement length requirement length requirement length requirement)
  - Oh this looks stunning.. thr color grading in the 70s was perfect. The seaside small town setting reminds me a bit of The Fog, which should probably also go on the list now that I think about it
6. It's not one of his most highly regarded films, but I love John Carpenter's The Fog which definitely fits the bill with the name. Debatably not a horror as well, but Seven is horrific and always makes me think of torrential, miserable rain.
7. I can recommend The Ritual. It's a folklore horror set in Norway and the weather plays a part in it to a decent degree. I rewatch it pretty often, it is one of my go-to horror movies of the last decade or so.
8. The first one that comes to mind is The Others but I see that it's already on your list. You could also check our The Wailing (lots of atmosphere and rain) and Coherence, which is more of a cosmic horror-themed Black Mirror episode. 
  - I love the others, it’s so cold without being overly desaturated the way a lot of horror movies are. It can come off as a bit lazy sometimes. The Skeleton Key has a similar vibe. The Wailing was amazing but >!so depressing lol!<

## 5. Sorry if this has come up a lot but I wanted to discuss the character Morpheus from Matrix Resurrections. What do you think this character symbolizes? How is he similar or different from Morpheus Uno?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** So the opening scene is a copy of the opening of the first movie except Bugs is watching it play out. They are in a small program called a modal. Bugs remarks that this is something they’ve seen before but also different. She’s talking about what’s happening in the modal but also explaining how this movie works( this movie is very meta). Anyways the first time we see Yahya he is playing the role of Smith from the first movie. Black suit , your men are already dead and all that jazz.

Eventually after some fighting Bugs seems trapped by the agents but is saved by”Smith”. After some discussion “Smith“ proclaims that he is Morpheus. He accepts the red pill as he sees it as a symbol of truth and together they set out to find Neo.

The most popular reading of this is probably that this is a trans allegory because the world sees him as Smith but he makes up his mind that his true self is Morpheus. I’ve also seen the character compared to Finn from Star Wars who is a stormtrooper who defects to the resistance. Notice Morpheus and Bugs share some resemblance to Finn and Rose. The pairs “side quest“ was one of the most criticized parts of the Star Wars sequels. As much as the movie is about sequels this could make sense. Additionally he has been compared to the apostle Paul. Paul was a prosecutor of early Christians but later became one of the most important voices of the church. Something like Smith was hunting humans but something changed in him on the road to da Matrix. There is a history of Neo being compared to the story of Christ so maybe there is something there.

Anyways Morpheus confronts Neo for the first time and he explains that he was in fact created by Neo. That it was his attempt to create his own savior or someone who could free him from this Matrix. I like to imagine this meaning that Lana wrote the character of Morpheus and the modal as a small experiment but the idea and that character were powerful enough that she was able to go through with the entire project.

Finally after Neo is freed from the pod we see Morpheus on the ship. In the real world he has a physical presence by use of some magnetic tech. He has fully transformed from the idea Neo implanted into the modal to a physical being in the real world.
